coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent N, S-co-doped carbon dots and their potential applications as antioxidants and sensitive probes for Cr (VI) detection
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Jie
Shang, Shaoming
Chen, Xiuying
Wang, Dan
Cai, Yan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a facile synthesis of N, S-co-doped carbon dots (N, SCDs) from biomass was developed by hydrothermal treatment, using ammonium persulfate as passivator. The N, SCDs have favorable fluorescent properties including high quantum yield (18.7%) and idea fluorescent stability. In addition, the N, SCDs exhibit excellent radical scavenging activities against DPPH radicals, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anion radicals. It is found that the fluorescence of N, SCDs can be effectively quenched by Cr (VI). The quenching mechanism could be explained due to the antioxidation of N, SCDs. Based on this feature, the selective and sensitive detection of Cr (VI) was performed, showing that a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of N, SCDs and Cr (VI) was achieved at the concentration from 1 to 40μM. Furthermore, the detection was applied in real water samples (Taihu and tap waters), resulting in high sensitivity and good recoveries. In a word, this study provides more applications of N, SCDs in antioxidant fields and environmental fields for Cr (VI) detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Antioxidation
Cr (Ⅵ) detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Scavenging of superoxide anion free radicals
Fluorescence assay of Cr (VI) in real water samples
Sensitive and selective detection of Cr (VI)
The optimal pH for Cr (VI) detection
Selectivity of N, SCDs for Cr (VI) detection
Sensitivity of N, SCDs for Cr (VI) detection
Application of Cr (VI) detection in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, SCDs, (b) size distribution of N, SCDs.
(a) XPS spectra of the N,SCDs; high resolution (b) C1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N1s, and (e) S2p peaks of the N,SCDs.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of N, SCDs, inset picture shows the N, SCDs under daylight (left) and UV light at 365nm wavelength (right), (b) PL excitation and emission spectra, (c) PL emission spectra of the N, SCDs different excitation wavelengths..
Scavenging activities of N,SCDs against (a) DPPH radicals, (b) hydroxyl radicals, (c) superoxide anion radicals.
The effect of pH on the fluorescent intensity of N, SCDs before and after adding K2Cr2O7.

(a) Fluorescence responds of N, SCDs (60μM) towards different metal ions (150μM); (b) Selective fluorescence response of N, SCDs (60μM) towards K2Cr2O7 (50μM, red bars) and interference of different metal ions (150μM, blue bars). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N, SCDs (60μg/mL) upon addition of various concentrations of Cr(VI) (from top to button:0, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500μM), (b) the relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and concentration of Cr(VI), inset shows a linear relationsip of (F0-F)/F0 versus the concentration of Cr(VI) from 1 to 40μM.
Comparison of different CDs for Cr(VI) assay.

row.entry: Cu, N, S, C, O, H

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Cr(VI) in Taihu water.
Detection of Cr(VI) in Tap water.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent N, S-co-doped carbon dots and their potential applications as antioxidants and sensitive probes for Cr (VI) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nantong University, Nantong 226019, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a facile synthesis of N, S-co-doped carbon dots (N, SCDs) from biomass was developed by hydrothermal treatment, using ammonium persulfate as passivator. The N, SCDs have favorable fluorescent properties including high quantum yield (18.7%) and idea fluorescent stability. In addition, the N, SCDs exhibit excellent radical scavenging activities against DPPH radicals, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anion radicals. It is found that the fluorescence of N, SCDs can be effectively quenched by Cr (VI). The quenching mechanism could be explained due to the antioxidation of N, SCDs. Based on this feature, the selective and sensitive detection of Cr (VI) was performed, showing that a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of N, SCDs and Cr (VI) was achieved at the concentration from 1 to 40μM. Furthermore, the detection was applied in real water samples (Taihu and tap waters), resulting in high sensitivity and good recoveries. In a word, this study provides more applications of N, SCDs in antioxidant fields and environmental fields for Cr (VI) detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive attention since they were first discovered during the treatment of single-wall carbon nanotubes [1]. As a new kind of carbon-based materials, carbon dots possess many unique properties such as excellent and stable fluorescence, good water solubility, low toxicity and satisfying biocompatibility [2–5]. These advantages have drawn much research engaging in the exploration of new CDs with improving properties, together with their potential applications. In particular, fluorescence is one of the outstanding properties of CDs because it helps CDs become desirable fluorescent probes for imaging [2,5–7], photocatalysis [8,9] and detection in various fields [10–14]. Thus, it is valuable to develop highly and stably fluorescent CDs through facile pathway. Recently, hydrothermal treatment is extensively employed in the preparation of CDs [15–17]. In contrast to other methods, this method has superior characteristics that are not only simple, economical but also efficient and eco-friendly [18–20]. Therefore it facilitates to produce different fluorescent CDs in large scale, providing the guarantee for the subsequently wide application.

Nowadays, many efforts are paid on the synthesis of CDs doped with heteroatom, especially the N and S elements for the sake of the desirable fluorescence [21–23]. For instance, Zhang et al. [24] employed cysteine and citric acid to fabricate S, N co-doped CDs by hydrothermal synthesis. The result showed that these CDs have stable and bright blue emission with a quantum yield (QY) of 54%. Wang et al. [25] respectively chosen glucose as precursor, ammonium persulfate and ethylenediamine as passivator to prepare N,S-codoped fluorescent CDs with the QY as high as 21.6%. Besides compounds, biomass is also been considered as raw materials to synthesize CDs. Zhao et al. [26] directly applied garlic as carbon precursor to synthesize CDs with QY of 17.6%. It is found that N and S elements existing in garlic were reacted onto the surface of CDs in the presence of functional groups, indicating that they play an important role in enhancing fluorescence of CDs. It is known that biomass is plenty of carbohydrates that supply amounts of carbon source for the preparation of CDs. Meanwhile, the biomass also contains proteins and other molecules that provide heteroatoms including N and S elements. Thus, it seems that biomass can be self-passivated without any extra passivator during the carbonization. To some extent, it agrees well with the literatures that the heteroatoms in biomass exist on the surface of CDs during carbonization [27]. However, in most cases only N element can participate in the synthesis [4,28,29]. Fewer studies have reported that other heteroatoms including S element from biomass are successfully reacted on the surface of CDs [26]. The reason may be explained that compared with C, H, O and N elements, the content of S element is very small, leading to the quite little chance for S element to participate in the synthesis. On the other hand, the compounds including S element from biomass may be difficult to transfer S element onto the surface of CDs. Obviously, it is urgent to search a new pathway to solve this problems.

As for the applications of CDs, many investigations take great interest in detection due to the fluorescent quenching effect. Yan et al. [30] developed an “off-on” approach to detect Hg2+ and L-cysteine based on their new synthesized CDs. Han et al. [31] demonstrated their CDs applying in detecting Fe3+ and apoferritin. In addition, Tang et al. [32] suggested that the beta-cyclodextrin-modified CDs can be employed to detect alkaline phosphatase activity. Besides fluorescence, the antioxidation of CDs are gradually been found recently [33–35]. Sachdev et al. [33] prepared CDs from coriander leaves, showing that they have the ability to scavenge DPPH radicals. Zhao et al. [26] found that their synthesized CDs perform favorable scavenging DPPH radicals activity, while they have poor capability in scavenging OH radicals. As a newly discovered property, antioxidation of CDs is rarely reported and is mainly focused on the scavenging of DPPH radicals. Thus, it is meaningful to develop novel CDs that can scavenge various free radicals, broadening their application in antioxidant fields.

In this study, we combined biomass and compound to synthesize fluorescent N, S co-doped CDs (N, SCDs) through hydrothermal treatment. Pomelo, a kind of common fruit was served as carbon source while ammonium persulfate was employed as passivator to provide N and S elements. The characterization and fluorescent properties of N, SCDs were discussed in detail. Furthermore, the antioxidation of the N, SCDs was explored and investigated, indicating that these N, SCDs are hopeful to expand their application in antioxidant fields. In addition, the fluorescence of the obtained N, SCDs can be quenched by Cr (VI) in concentration dependent manner, showing that these CDs are promising in sensitive detection of Cr (VI) that are harmful to health and environment. Subsequently, The N, SCDs were successfully applied to detect the amount of Cr (VI) in real water samples.

Fresh pomelo was purchased from local market. CuSO4, BaCl2, FeCl3, CaCl2, Zn(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, NiCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, MnSO4, (CH3COO)2Cd·2H2O, Al2(SO4)3, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl2, AgNO3, HgCl2, CrPO4, K2Cr2O7, H3PO4, diphenylcarbazide and ascorbic acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The quinine sulfate was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water of which conductivity is 18.2MΩcm−1 was purified by the Millipore water purification system before being used in the experiments.

The N, SCDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment using pomelo as carbon source and ammonium persulfate as passivator, respectively. Briefly, the pulp of fresh pomelo was blended by high speed tissue blender (12,000r/min, 6min) to harvest juice. Then, the juice was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 20min to discard precipitation. 40mL of pomelo juce was mixed with 6g of ammonium persulfate. After the ammonium persulfate was absolutely dissolved, the mixture was transferred into a 50mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated constantly at 200°C for 12h. The mixture was cooled down naturally after reaction. Subsequently, the obtained dark solution was centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 15min to remove large particles, and then filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane. Finally, the solution was dialyzed against deionized water for 48h with a dialysis tube of which molecular weight cut off was 3500. The solvent was removed by freezing-drying and the N, SCDs in powder was collected.

Fluorescence spectra were acquired by a Shimadzu RF–5301 spectrofluorimeter. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a TU–1991 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured by a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrophotometer. Trans-mission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope using an acceleration voltage of 200kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained by a Bruker D2-phaser diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a VG ESCALAB 220i-XL surface analysis system.

The quantum yield of the N, SCDs was calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 and its QY is 54%. The QY was calculated as follows:
QYS =QYST (IS/IST)(AST/AS)(ηS/ηST)2
Where the subscript “S” and “ST” respectively stand for CDs and quinine sulfate; “I” refers to integrated PL intensity; “A” is the optical density; “η” represents the refractive index of the solvent (both ηS and ηST are 1.33). In order to minimize the reabsorption effect, the absorption intensity was kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength of 380nm.

The scavenging of DPPH free radicals was estimated to study the antioxidant property of the N, SCDs. DPPH was dissolved in absolute ethanol at the concentration of 100μM. 10μL of N, SCDs solution with different concentration was added in 5mL of DPPH solution. After incubation for 30min in dark, the absorbance was tested at 517nm. The scavenging of DPPH free radicals was measured as follows:
% scavenging activity=(Ac–As)/Ac
Where Ac and As refer to the absorbance of the DPPH free radicals at 517nm in the absence and presence of N, SCDs, respectively.

The scavenging of hydroxyl free radicals was determined according to fenton reaction [36]. Briefly, 2mL of 1.8mM FeSO4 was mixed with 1.5mL of 1.8mM salicylic acid ethanol solution, followed by the addition of 1.4mL of N, SCDs with different concentration. Finally, 0.1mL of 100mM H2O2 was added into the whole solution. The reaction was conducted for 5min at 37°C. The absorbance was recorded at 510nm. The scavenging of hydroxyl free radicals was calculated using the following equation:
% scavenging activity=(A0–As)/A0
Where A0 and As refer to the absorbance of the hydroxyl free radicals at 510nm in the absence and presence of N, SCDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scavenging of superoxide anion free radicals
ce:section.ce:para:
The capability of N, SCDs to scavenge superoxide anion free radicals was performed at 25°C. 3.48mL of 50mM Tris-HCl buffer with pH 8.2 was mixed with 0.83mL of N, SCDs with diverse concentration. Then the solution was reacted with 0.33mL of 0.01M HCl containing 10mM pyrogallic acid. In addition, the rate of reaction was controlled as the OD value was in the range of 0.05–0.06per min. After 4min’ reaction, the absorbance was immediately detected at 325nm. The scavenging of superoxide anion free radicals was evaluated as follows:
% scavenging activity=(A’–As)/A’
Where A’ and As refer to the absorbance of the superoxide anion free radicals at 325nm in the absence and presence of N, SCDs, respectively.

The fluorescence detection of Cr (VI) was performed in pH 3 environment at room temperature. In a typical assay, a certain concentration of K2Cr2O7 was added into N, SCDs solution of which final concentration was kept at 60μg/mL. The mixture was detected after 30min. The sensitivity and selectivity for Cr (VI) was also investigated by adding 150μM other ions as the concentration of Cr (VI) was set at 100μM. The fluorescent spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380nm. All the measurements were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of Cr (VI) in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, two kinds of drinking water, the river water and tap water were chosen for the analysis of real samples. Moreover, the river water was obtained from Taihu River in Wuxi, China. The river water was first filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane and then centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min to remove any suspended particles. The tap water was boiling for 30min before use. Both the two real samples were adjusted to pH 3. The procedure for the assay of Cr (VI) was the same as mentioned above.

The morphology and size of N, S-CDs was imaged by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , the N, SCDs are nearly spherical and highly monodispersed that no aggregation was found. The diameters of the N, SCDs are in the range of 5.5–8.7nm with the average diameter of 6.7nm, indicating that these N, SCDs are nearly uniform in size. The HRTEM image shows the clear lattice structure with spacing of 0.21nm. This situation is similar to that of graphitic carbon, indicating the high crystallinity of N,SCDs [34]. The XRD pattern of N, SCDs revealed a low-intensity reflection centered at 20° (Fig. S1), attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms. FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 ) was used to identify the functional groups on the CDs. An absorption band at 3423cm−1 is due to the NH stretching vibration; a band at 3244cm−1 represents the OH stretching vibration; the peaks at 2923 and 2858cm−1 are assigned to CH stretching vibration; the peak centered at 1710cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching vibration; the peak at 1627cm−1 indicates the CN stretching vibration; the peak at 1398cm−1 is ascribed to CN stretching vibration; the peak at 1114cm−1 shows the existence of −SO3 or C=S; the peak at 617cm−1 shows the CS group. According to the FTIR spectrum, it is shown that N and S elements are doped in the CDs. The N, SCDs are characterized using XPS in order to explore their elemental composition and chemical bonds. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 3 (a) reveals that the N, SCDs comprise C, O, N and S elements of which ratio of aromatic contents were 73.14%, 19.38%, 4.26% and 3.22%, respectively. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 3(b)) can be resolved into 4 peaks with binding energy at 284.8, 286.0, 287.1 and 289.1ev. These peaks are corresponding to CC/CC, CS/CO, CO and CN bonds, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) is deconvoluted into 3 peaks at 398.7, 399.8, and 402.1ev, which are assigned to NC, CNC, and NH bonds, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) is fitted with 3 peaks at 533.8, 532.5 and 531.7ev, which is attributed to OCO, COH and CO, respectively. In addition, the S 2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 3(e), indicating that S element is present in C-SOx (x=2–4) species of which peaks are centered at 168–169ev [37]. The synthesis of CDs without ammonium persulfate was performed in this study. From Fig. 2S, it is shown that the there are no N or S in the CDs. Thus, it is confirmed the role of ammonium persulfate which supplies N and S elements.

The mechanism of the formation of the CDs especially using biomass as precursor is still unclear. Because the intermediates and products are very complex, it is hard to separate and identify those compounds definitely. Here, a possible pathway of the formation of N, S-CDs is discussed in detail[38,39]. The macromolecular carbohydrates from pomelo are first decomposed into monosaccharaides such as glucose. Then, these monosaccharaides are dehydrated to form furfural intermediates and subsequently the polymerization and condensation take place to generate aromatic and polymeric products. After carbonation, the CDs are obtained by a probable nuclear burst of aromatic cluster at critical concentration and supersaturation point. In addition, the N, S elements are doped in the initial stages through amidation and sulfonation reaction.

Fig. 4 (a) depicted the UV–vis spectrum of N, SCDs, showing that an obvious absorption band at 283nm is attributed to n-π* transition of CO bond. In addition, the N, SCDs solution exhibits bright blue light under UV irradiation of 365nm. As it can be seen in Fig. 4(b) the maximum emission wavelength is located at 450nm as the excitation wavelength is 380nm. Fig. 4(c) demonstrated the relationship between excitation and emission wavelength. As the previous literatures reported [31–33], the emission wavelength is red-shifted with the increasing excitation wavelength. This excitation wavelength-dependent behavior could be illustrated due to the different sizes or diverse surface emissive trap sites. The QY of the N, S-CDs is estimated as 18.7%, which is higher than those of CDs that were only derived from biomass in most cases.

Although it is a great challenge for researchers to disclose the fluorescence mechanism of CDs, an acceptable perspective has been proposed recently. It is suggested that the fluorescence feature of CDs is related to their surface energy trap, electron-hole recombination, quantum size effect, aromatic structures and edge effect [40]. Furthermore, doping heteroatoms is feasible to change these factors above, resulting in the improvement of QY. Wang et al. [41] survey that the introduction of N, S elements is more likely affect surface trap. Moreover, they suggested that the introduction of S elements in the form of CS on the surface of their N, SCDs contributes to high QY. Wang et al. [42] revealed that N-doping is benefit to form conjugated π electron system, implying that hybridization between the molecular state and carbon backbone plays an important role in the fluorescence mechanism. In our work, It has been identified the existence of CS and CN bands, indicating that they make contribution to the satisfying QY according to the discussion above. To further confirm the importance of the doping N and S elements to the QY of N, S CDs. The QY of the CDs only prepared from pomelo juice was calculated, revealing that the QY was just 1.3%. Therefore, it is worthy to doping the heteroatoms to improve the fluorescent properties of CDs.

The effect of ion strength on N, SCDs was employed to estimate fluorescence stability. As it can be seen in Fig. S3, the intensity has slight fluctuation as the concentration of NaCl is increasing up to 3M. Thus, it is obvious that the N, SCDs has excellent optical stability under a high ionic strength environment. Moreover, the influence of pH on fluorescence stability was also studied, which will be discussed in the following paragraph.

In order to detect the antioxidant activity of N, SCDs, three kinds of typical free radicals (DPPH radicals, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anion radicals) were chosen as oxidants in this study. Here, 5μg/mL ascorbic acid was used as positive control, showing that the scavenging of DPPH radicals, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anion radicals were 45%, 15% and 88%, respectively. As it can be seen in Fig. 5 , the N, SCDs present good antioxidant activity in scavenging theses free radicals. DPPH is a stable free radical due to the N with lone pair electron surrounded by three benzene rings. When it is reacted with antioxidant, its color changes from deep violet to light yellow, and the absorption at 517nm was weaken [43]. As Fig. 5(a) indicated, the antioxidant activity of N, SCDs were increased with the concentration up to 350μg/mL. As the concentration continued increasing, the antioxidant activity rose gradually and at last came into saturation at the concentration of 500μg/mL. Finally, the maximal DPPH radical scavenging activity of N, SCDs was as estimated as 56%. Hydroxyl radical is another free radical with strong oxidation ability. Here, the hydroxyl radical was generated by fenton reaction using Fe2+ and H2O2 as reactants [36]. Then, the antioxidant activity was evaluated under the absorbance at 510nm. The result showed that the antioxidant activity of N, SCDs has dose-dependent behavior (Fig. 5(b)). In the case of the concentration more than 500μg/mL, the antioxidant activity trended to increase progressively. The hydroxyl radical scavenging activity of N, SCDs was as high as 58% at the concentration of 800μg/mL. Superoxide anion radicals are the earliest discovered free radicals and have the largest number among all the free radicals in human body. It is suggested that the superoxide anion radicals can induce the lipid peroxidation, leading to the accelerating aging and diseases especially cancer [44]. Therefore, it is significant to explore an antioxidant that can effectively scavenge superoxide anion radicals. In this study, superoxide anion radical was detected based on pyrogallol autoxidation method. From Fig. 5(c), an idea result showed that the scavenging superoxide anion radical activity of N, SCDs was up to 39% only at the concentration of 25μg/mL. As the concentration of N, SCDs continued increasing, the antioxidant activity improved slower. The antioxidant activity trended to saturate when the concentration exceeded 400μg/mL. Finally, the antioxidant activity of N, SCDs was as high as 81% at the concentration of 700μg/mL. Compared with the antioxidant activity of N, SCDs towards scavenging the three free radicals, it is concluded that N, SCDs have similar ability to scavenge DPPH and hydroxyl radicals. Meanwhile, it is worth noting that N, SCDs has outstanding ability in scavenging superoxide anion radicals.

Moreover, to further explore the antioxidation of N, SCDs, KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 were employed to study the effect of oxidants on the fluorescence quenching of N, SCDs (Fig. S4). In this work, the N, SCDs together with KMnO4 or K2Cr2O7 are in acid environment (pH 3). The concentrations of N, SCDs, KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 were respectively 60, 30 and 100μM. It is suggested in Fig. S4 that KMnO4 could dramatically quench the fluorescence of N, SCDs. Meanwhile, K2Cr2O7 also had great effect on fluorescence quenching. Obviously, KMnO4 had more significantly quenching influence than K2Cr2O7. In this condition, according to Nernst equation, the electrode potentials of KMnO4 and K2Cr2O7 are estimated as 1.22 and 0.92V respectively, showing that KMnO4 has stronger oxidation activity than K2Cr2O7. Thus, it can be indicated that the stronger oxidant has more ability to quench the fluorescence of N, SCDs. This phenomenon agrees well with what in literature [34]. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. S5 (b) revealed that the absorption curve of KMnO4 and N, SCDs complex is significantly different from those of KMnO4 and N, SCDs. From the absorption curve of KMnO4 and N, SCDs complex, a new little peak at 420nm was observed, confirming that new functional group was generated. As for the UV–vis absorption spectra of K2Cr2O7, N, SCDs and their complex, it is shown that the absorption peak of the complex was shifted with 46nm compared with that of K2Cr2O7. Therefore, it could be indicated that the K2Cr2O7 and N, SCDs also had reacted, probably producing new functional group. In addition, the concentrations of Cr(VI) (5, 10 15μM) before and after the addition of N, SCDs were tested based on photometry of diphenylcarbazide (Fig. S6). It is found that the absorbance values of the mixtures containing N, SCDs and Cr(VI) were zero. Thus, it is indicated that a redox reaction was conducted between N, SCDs and Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Cr (VI)
The optimal pH for Cr (VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of hazardous heavy metal ion, Cr (VI) has been received extensive attention because it widely exists in industrial waste water. Based on the fact that K2Cr2O7 can quench the fluorescence of N, SCDs, we attempt to apply N, SCDs as fluorescent probes for Cr (VI) detection. Since pH plays an important role both in fluorescence of N,SCDs and the form of Cr (VI), the responds of fluorescence to pH was firstly studied. As Fig. 6 suggested, before adding K2Cr2O7, the intensity of fluorescence of N, SCDs is increasing as the pH value rose from 1 to 3. Then the intensity of fluorescence almost kept the same in the case of the pH value in the range of 3–10. As the pH value continued increasing, the intensity of fluorescence decreased. Wang et al. [3] suggested that increasing the pH value could result in the aggregations of CDs, consequently decreasing the intensity of fluorescence. Furthermore, many literatures are agreeable that this pH dependent fluorescence can be illustrated due to the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of CDs [10,14,34]. After adding K2Cr2O7, the intensity of fluorescence decreased dramatically. Compared with the difference between fluorescent intensities before and after adding K2Cr2O7 at the same pH value, it is found that the quenching effect is more obvious in acid condition. This phenomenon may arise from the existing form of Cr (VI). In other words, Cr (VI) is dominantly in the form of Cr2O7 2− in acid condition and CrO4 2− in base environment. It is known that Cr2O7 2− has stronger oxidation in acid solution, thus it is favorable to enable Cr2O7 2− to take part in oxidation-reduction reaction.

As Fig. 6 mentioned, the initially maximum fluorescent intensity of N, SCDs was obtained at pH3 before adding Cr2O7 2−. At this pH, the fluorescence of N, SCDs was most severely quenched after the addition of Cr (VI) ascribed to the good sensitivity. Therefore, pH3 was chosen as the optimal value in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N, SCDs for Cr (VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the selectivity of N, SCDs and interference to Cr (VI) detection, 16 common ions at the concentration of 150μM were applied in the experiments. It is shown in Fig. 7 (a), in the presence of N, SCDs of 60μM, most ions have no or negligible quenching effect. However, Fe3+ showed relatively stronger fluorescent quenching. It can be explained that Fe3+ combines with the OH on the surface of CDs, results in a nonradiative electron transfer from the excited state of the N-CDs to Fe3+ [45]. This phenomenon further confirms the existence of OH and COOH groups that have been previously characterized. In addition, as Fig. 7(b) mentioned that the fluorescence of N, SCDs was quenched dramatically upon the addition of 100μM Cr (VI) in the presence of the ions of 150μM. A satisfying result showed that the interfering ions including Fe3+ have nearly no interference to fluorescence intensity. In addition, the tolerance molar ratio of Fe3+ to Cr (VI) was evaluated as 4. Therefore, it is indicated that N, SCDs are promising for Cr (VI) detection. As for the Cr (VI) detection in real water sample which has Fe3+, adding NaF into the sample can effectively shield the interference (Fig. S7). Moreover, the excessive amount of NaF has negligible effect on the fluorescent quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of N, SCDs for Cr (VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of N, SCDs are recorded after the addition of different concentrations of Cr (VI). It is shown (Fig. 8 (a)) that the intensity of fluorescence centered at 450nm decreased gradually with the increasing concentration of Cr (VI) from 0 to 500μM. It is reported previously that this fluorescent quenching is due to the inner filter effect of Cr2O7 2− [46]. That is to say, the absorption of Cr2O7 2− shields the excitation or emission wavelengths of CDs, leading their fluorescence quenching [47]. This suggestion can be employed to explain the fluorescence response to our N, SCDs that the broad absorption of Cr2O7 2− overlapped the excitation and emission bands of N, SCDs. Moreover, in terms of the antioxidation of N, SCDs, redox reaction may be another reason for the fluorescence quenching. Fig. 8(b) presents the relationship between the quenching ratio (F0-F)/F0 (F0and F are fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Cr2O7 2−, respectively) and Cr (hields the excitation or emission wavelengt) concentration. A good linear correlation exists over the range of Cr (VI) concentration from 1 to 40μM with the correlation efficient (R2) of 0.9946. The limit of detection was calculated as 0.52μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD=3σ/S, where σ represents the standard deviation of 11 blank measurements, S is the slope of the calibration curve). According to the World Health Organization(WHO), the concentration of Cr (VI) should be controlled below 50μg/L (0.96μM) [48]. It is noticeable that using N, SCDs as probes is sensitive enough to detect Cr (VI) in water. Compare with the other CDs based fluorescent sensing systems for Cr (VI) detection (Table 1 ), the lowest LOD of 1.49×10−3 was reported [53]. However, the corresponding linear range of Cr (VI) concentration was only from 0 to 6μM, which limits the quantitative analysis in higher concentration. On the other hand, the N,SCDs for Cr (VI) detection have comparatively lower LOD but broader linear range, which have more potential in monitoring Cr (VI) concentration. In addition, it is suggested that the N, SCDs are metal free that are safer to be applied in biological fields. As it is shown that the CDs prepared from citric acid result in high QY, which has been proposed in the previous study. As Table 1 indicated, the N, SCDs with satisfying QY for Cr (VI) detection was the first reported using biomass as carbon source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Cr (VI) detection in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a facile synthesis of N, S-co-doped carbon dots was developed using pomelo as carbon source and ammonium persulfate as passivator, respectively. The characterization was carried out to confirm the existing of N and S elements on the surface of CDs. The obtained N, SCDs exhibit satisfying fluorescent properties with QY of 18.7%. In addition, three kinds of typical free radicals (DPPH radicals, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide anion radicals) were employed to investigate the antioxidation of N, SCDs. The results showed that the N, SCDs have favorable ability to scavenge these free radicals. Especially, the scavenging superoxide anion radical activity of N, SCDs was as high as 81%. In addition, the N, SCDs showed excellent sensitivity and high selectivity for Cr (VI) detection with a detection limit lower than that proposed by WHO. Subsequently, the N, SCDs are successfully applied to detect the amount of Cr (VI) in Taihu and tap water samples with acceptable recoveries. This study broadens the application of CDs both in antioxidant field and sensing platform in Cr (VI) detection in environmental water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP11517), Youth Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20150401).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.03.123.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jie Shen received her PHD degree in applied chemistry from Jiangnan University in 2014. Now she is working as a lecture in Jinagnan University. Her research is focused on preparation of nano-materials, biotechnology and analytical chemistry.

Shaoming Shang received his B.S. degree in analytical chemistry from Xiamen University in 1982. He received his M.S degree in analytical chemistry from Jiangnan University in 2000. He is an professor in Jiangnan University. His research fields include analytical chemistry and inorganic chemistry.

Xiuying Chen received her PHD degree in applied chemistry from Dalian University of Technology in 2006. Currently, she is an associate professor in Jiangnan Universty. Her scientific interests focused on the development of fluorescent probes based on nano-materials and organic chemical synthesis.

Dan Wang received his Ph.D. degree in optical engineering from Zhejiang University, China, in 2013. Then he joined Prof. Liming Dai's group as a Visiting Scholar at Case Western Reserve University in USA. He has been an Associate Professor at Beijing University of Chemical Technology in China since 2015. His research interests focus on green synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and their applications.

Yan Cai received her PHD degree in applied chemistry from Jiangnan University in 2010. She has joined in Nantong University as an associate professor. Now her research interests focused on biochemistry and analytical chemistry.
